threats.title: RIP-7212 Precompile False Positive on Chains with Arbitrary Code at Address 0x100
threats.hypothesis_description: WebAuthn.verify performs a staticcall to address 0x100 (VERIFIER) for RIP-7212 precompile signature verification. If no code exists at 0x100, staticcall returns success=true with empty returndata, and the code falls through to FCL (safe). However, on target chains where a regular contract exists at address 0x100 (any of: Ethereum, Polygon, BNB, Avalanche, Gnosis), the staticcall may return arbitrary data. If ret.length > 0 and abi.decode(ret, (uint256)) == 1, the function returns true immediately, accepting any forged passkey signature without falling through to FCL verification.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code checks `ret.length > 0` and `abi.decode(ret, (uint256)) == 1` but does not verify that the address contains the actual RIP-7212 precompile vs arbitrary code. On any target chain where 0x100 has deployed bytecode that happens to return 32 bytes decoding to 1 (or can be made to), all passkey-owner signatures would be trivially forgeable, enabling complete wallet takeover.
deployment_assumptions: RIP-7212 precompile is correctly deployed at 0x100 on all target chains, OR no contract code exists at 0x100
threats.config_evidence: Target chains include Ethereum, Polygon, BNB, Avalanche, Gnosis per documentation. No deployment evidence verifying 0x100 status on each chain.

proof_of_signal.pattern: staticcall to hardcoded VERIFIER=0x100 with only ret.length>0 check; no validation that 0x100 is actually RIP-7212 precompile vs arbitrary contract

preconditions: Target chain has deployed contract code at address 0x100 that is NOT the RIP-7212 precompile
Said contract returns 32+ bytes of data that decodes to uint256(1) when called with the abi.encode(messageHash, r, s, x, y) payload
Wallet has at least one passkey (secp256r1) owner registered

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Trusting staticcall return data from unverified address
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: bool valid = ret.length > 0; if (success && valid) return abi.decode(ret, (uint256)) == 1; — falls through to FCL only if precompile returns empty data or call fails
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No chain-specific check for RIP-7212 deployment, no code-size check on VERIFIER address

analysis_questions: On which target chains does address 0x100 have deployed code?
What does the contract at 0x100 return when called with the 5-word ABI-encoded payload?
Could an attacker deploy code at 0x100 on any target chain via CREATE2?
validation_requirements: Check code size at 0x100 on all 8 target chains
Test staticcall behavior with forged signature data on chains with code at 0x100
Verify FCL fallback is never reached when precompile returns exploitable data

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker identifies a target chain where 0x100 returns favorable data. They craft a WebAuthnAuth struct with arbitrary r,s values, submit a UserOp signed with this forged passkey signature, validateUserOp accepts it via the WebAuthn path, and the attacker gains full wallet control to drain all funds.
prerequisites: Contract code at 0x100 on target chain
Passkey owner registered on victim wallet

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete wallet takeover — all ETH, ERC-20 tokens, and NFTs drained
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All wallets using passkey owners on affected chains
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None — attacker can also add their own owner and remove existing ones

threats.test_coverage_gap: No cross-chain deployment verification for VERIFIER address

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Difficult to detect proactively without chain-specific audits

threats.title: No Minimum Owner Count Allows Permanent Wallet Bricking via Owner Removal
threats.hypothesis_description: removeOwnerAtIndex has no check to ensure at least one owner remains. A single owner (or the last remaining owner) can remove ALL owners including themselves. With zero owners, no one can call any onlyOwner function (addOwner, execute, upgrade), permanently locking all funds with no recovery mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: The removeOwnerAtIndex function only checks that an owner exists at the given index (NoOwnerAtIndex), deletes it, and emits an event. There is no counter tracking active owners and no minimum-count enforcement. After all owners are removed, _checkOwner always reverts (isOwnerAddress returns false for any address, and msg.sender != address(this) for external calls).

proof_of_signal.pattern: removeOwnerAtIndex deletes owner without checking remaining owner count; no activeOwnerCount state variable exists

preconditions: Caller is a current owner of the wallet
Caller knows the indices of all current owners

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Owner removal without minimum count enforcement
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: delete _getMultiOwnableStorage().isOwner[owner] and delete ownerAtIndex[index] with no minimum-1 check
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No active owner counter, no require(remainingOwners >= 1) check

analysis_questions: Can the last owner remove themselves via a single transaction?
Is there any other code path that could re-add an owner after all are removed?
Could the EntryPoint path somehow bypass the owner check to add a new owner?
validation_requirements: Test removing all owners sequentially
Verify that no function can be called after all owners are removed
Confirm funds are permanently locked

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A compromised owner key holder calls removeOwnerAtIndex for every active owner index (including their own). After the last removal, the wallet has zero owners, and all onlyOwner/onlyEntryPointOrOwner functions revert, permanently locking all ETH, tokens, and NFTs.
prerequisites: At least one owner key is compromised or malicious

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of all wallet funds
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All wallet co-owners and the wallet itself
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None — no function can add new owners without owner authorization

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for zero-owner state reachability and fund recovery

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Minimal — N removeOwnerAtIndex calls where N is owner count

threats.title: WebAuthn authenticatorData Minimum Length Not Validated Before Flag Access
threats.hypothesis_description: WebAuthn.verify accesses webAuthnAuth.authenticatorData[32] to check UP and UV flags without first verifying that authenticatorData has at least 33 bytes. If authenticatorData is shorter than 33 bytes, the access causes an out-of-bounds revert rather than a graceful false return. This forces _validateSignature to revert instead of returning false, which has different semantic meaning in ERC-4337 validation.
threats.investigation_rationale: In validateUserOp, _validateSignature is expected to return true/false (0/1 validation result). A revert instead causes the entire validateUserOp to revert, making the UserOp unbundleable rather than just invalid. This could be used to cause permanent gas waste for bundlers if authenticatorData can be manipulated, though the authenticator controls this data.

proof_of_signal.pattern: webAuthnAuth.authenticatorData[32] accessed without length >= 33 check; out-of-bounds revert instead of returning false

preconditions: WebAuthnAuth struct contains authenticatorData shorter than 33 bytes
This struct is submitted as part of a UserOp signature or ERC-1271 call

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Direct array index access without bounds check
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: authenticatorData[32] reverts on short data instead of returning false; inconsistent with other validation checks that return false

analysis_questions: Can an attacker craft a UserOp with short authenticatorData that causes revert in validateUserOp?
Does the revert propagate differently than returning false/1 in ERC-4337 context?
Could this be used to grief bundlers by wasting their gas?
validation_requirements: Test WebAuthn.verify with authenticatorData of length 0, 1, 32
Verify the revert propagation through _validateSignature and validateUserOp
Compare bundler behavior for revert vs sigFailed=1 return

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker submits a UserOp with a WebAuthnAuth signature containing truncated authenticatorData (< 33 bytes). validateUserOp reverts instead of returning 1 (sig failure). Bundler wastes gas on simulation that reverts. Repeated submission could grief bundlers processing this wallet's UserOps.
prerequisites: Wallet has passkey owner
Attacker can submit UserOps to bundler

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Bundler gas waste; potential temporary denial of service for the wallet
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Bundlers processing UserOps for the wallet; wallet usability
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Full — legitimate UserOps with proper authenticatorData still work

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for short authenticatorData edge case

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Revert in validateUserOp visible to bundler

threats.title: Cross-Chain Replay of Owner Removal Enables Multi-Chain Wallet Bricking
threats.hypothesis_description: removeOwnerAtIndex is whitelisted in canSkipChainIdValidation. A signed owner removal UserOp is replayable across all chains. Combined with the lack of minimum owner count (TECH-H003), a single signed UserOp removing the last owner can be replayed to brick the wallet on ALL chains simultaneously. The attacker only needs to observe one chain's removal operation.
threats.investigation_rationale: If a wallet has one owner on all chains, and the owner legitimately removes themselves (e.g., during owner rotation with an add-before-remove that doesn't get replayed correctly), the removal UserOp can be replayed across all chains. Since addOwnerAddress and removeOwnerAtIndex are both replayable but use separate nonces, the replay ordering could result in the remove executing before the add on some chains, bricking those wallets.

proof_of_signal.pattern: removeOwnerAtIndex.selector returns true from canSkipChainIdValidation; combined with no minimum owner count in MultiOwnable

preconditions: Wallet has same address on multiple chains
Owner removal UserOp is executed via executeWithoutChainIdValidation
The removal leaves zero owners on at least one chain

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Replayable owner removal + no minimum owner enforcement
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Remove-last-owner UserOp replayed across all chains simultaneously bricks all instances
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No minimum owner count check in removeOwnerAtIndex, no per-chain ordering guarantee for cross-chain replays

analysis_questions: What happens if add-owner and remove-owner UserOps are replayed in different order across chains?
Is there a nonce mechanism preventing out-of-order replay?
Can the REPLAYABLE_NONCE_KEY's sequential enforcement prevent this?
validation_requirements: Test owner rotation (add then remove) with cross-chain replay
Verify nonce sequencing of replayable operations
Test scenario where remove reaches chain B before add

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: User on Chain A adds a new owner (UserOp1) then removes old owner (UserOp2), both via executeWithoutChainIdValidation. On Chain B, UserOp2 is replayed before UserOp1 (or UserOp1 fails for some reason). Old owner is removed on Chain B but new owner not yet added. If old owner was the only owner, wallet on Chain B is permanently bricked.
prerequisites: Cross-chain wallet
Owner rotation via replayable ops
Ordering asymmetry across chains

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of all wallet funds on affected chains
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Cross-chain wallet users performing owner management

threats.test_coverage_gap: No cross-chain owner rotation ordering test

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Depends on bundler inclusion timing across chains

threats.title: MagicSpend withdraw() Expiry Check After Nonce Consumption Creates Atomic Griefing on Direct Withdraw Path
threats.hypothesis_description: In MagicSpend.withdraw(), the order of operations is: (1) _validateRequest (consumes nonce), (2) isValidWithdrawSignature check, (3) expiry check, (4) _withdraw. While reverts in steps 2-3 roll back the nonce consumption (since it's the same transaction), the signature check in step 2 uses SignatureCheckerLib which can interact with external contracts if the MagicSpend owner is a contract (ERC-1271). If the MagicSpend owner is an ERC-1271 contract, a reentrancy path could potentially be exploited during the signature check to manipulate state before the expiry check.
threats.investigation_rationale: The MagicSpend owner could be a smart contract implementing ERC-1271 (SignatureCheckerLib.isValidSignatureNow supports this). During the signature check callback, the owner contract could perform arbitrary actions. While the MagicSpend contract has no reentrancy guards, the practical impact depends on whether the owner contract is adversarial — which conflicts with the trust model.
deployment_assumptions: MagicSpend owner is an EOA, not an ERC-1271 smart contract
threats.config_evidence: Constructor sets _owner via Ownable._initializeOwner. No evidence in config whether this is EOA or contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _validateRequest consumes nonce at line 182; isValidWithdrawSignature at line 184 calls SignatureCheckerLib which may invoke external ERC-1271 contract; expiry check at line 188 happens after external call

preconditions: MagicSpend owner is a smart contract implementing ERC-1271
The owner contract has exploitable callback behavior
Attacker can influence the owner contract's response during isValidSignatureNow

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: External call to owner contract (via SignatureCheckerLib) occurs between nonce consumption and expiry check
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No reentrancy guard on withdraw(); if owner is ERC-1271 contract, callback during signature check could re-enter
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No nonReentrant modifier, no checks-effects-interactions pattern for nonce vs external call

analysis_questions: Is the MagicSpend owner intended to be an EOA or could it be a multisig/smart contract?
What actions could a malicious ERC-1271 owner perform during the signature check callback?
Could the owner contract call back into MagicSpend.withdraw or withdrawGasExcess during the check?
validation_requirements: Deploy MagicSpend with ERC-1271 contract as owner
Attempt reentrancy during isValidWithdrawSignature callback
Verify state consistency if re-enter withdraw() or withdrawGasExcess()

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: MagicSpend owner is a smart contract. During withdraw(), the signature check calls the owner contract's isValidSignature. The owner contract (if malicious) could re-enter MagicSpend to manipulate _withdrawableETH or trigger additional withdrawals before the current one completes.
prerequisites: MagicSpend owner is smart contract with ERC-1271
Owner contract is malicious/compromised

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential double-withdrawal or state manipulation during re-entrance
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All MagicSpend users if owner contract is compromised
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Depends on extent of state corruption

threats.title: CoinbaseSmartWallet arbitrary-call reentrancy enables bypassing intended sequencing of owner changes/upgrade in the same execution context
threats.hypothesis_description: execute/executeBatch use raw .call to arbitrary targets with no reentrancy guard. If the wallet calls into an attacker-controlled contract, that contract can reenter back into wallet functions that accept msg.sender == address(this) as authorized (MultiOwnable._checkOwner allows self-calls). This can allow complex stateful sequences (e.g., owner removals/additions or upgrades) to be executed in unexpected order within a single outer call, potentially bypassing off-chain policy assumptions (e.g., “owner X must still exist when upgrade occurs”).
threats.investigation_rationale: While any owner already has broad powers, reentrancy can create surprising intra-tx ordering that breaks higher-level invariants or external integrations relying on linear execution. Because self-call is treated as owner, any reentrant call from wallet context becomes privileged if the attacker can get the wallet to call their contract during execute/executeBatch (common in token interactions).

proof_of_signal.lines: execute/executeBatch -> _call uses target.call{value:value}(data) with no reentrancy guard; MultiOwnable._checkOwner authorizes msg.sender==address(this)
proof_of_signal.pattern: unrestricted external call + privileged self-call authorization

preconditions: Wallet executes a call to an attacker-controlled contract (by owner intent or via a compromised dApp integration)
Attacker-controlled contract can call back into the wallet during the same transaction
Reentered function is gated by onlyOwner and accepts self-call as owner (true for MultiOwnable)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: reentrancy into privileged code paths via self-call trust
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: unexpected intra-transaction privilege escalation paths (from external call to privileged internal operations)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no nonReentrant or execution lock around execute/executeBatch

analysis_questions: Which privileged functions can be called successfully during reentrancy (addOwner/removeOwner/upgradeToAndCall)?
Are there any assumptions that 'only externally owned owners' call owner-management (broken by self-call path)?
Can reentrancy create a scenario where an owner is removed and then an upgrade is performed that off-chain systems would have disallowed?
validation_requirements: Build a malicious callee that reenters into owner-management and upgrade functions
Simulate wallet calling attacker contract via execute and observe owner set changes mid-call
Check whether UUPSUpgradeable implementation has its own reentrancy-sensitive checks

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Owner calls execute() to interact with a contract; that contract reenters wallet to call removeOwnerAtIndex/addOwnerAddress/upgradeToAndCall via address(this) privileged context, resulting in unexpected owner set or upgraded implementation within same tx.
prerequisites: A path where wallet is induced to call attacker contract (could be common DeFi interaction)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Could enable stealthy upgrade/owner-set mutation during benign-looking calls; potential full wallet compromise if combined with other logic flaws
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Wallet instances executing arbitrary external calls
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Depends; if upgraded maliciously, funds can be drained immediately

threats.test_coverage_gap: No explicit reentrancy/intra-tx sequencing tests for execute/executeBatch

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor unusual internal call patterns from wallet to itself during execution

threats.title: CoinbaseSmartWallet executeBatch unbounded calls array can be abused for gas-grief DoS against wallet operations
threats.hypothesis_description: executeBatch iterates over an unbounded calldata array and performs external calls for each element. A malicious (or compromised) owner / EntryPoint-submitted UserOp can craft extremely large batches that run out of gas or exceed block limits, potentially preventing intended batched execution or causing repeated failures that lock workflows relying on atomic batching.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a practical reliability issue for ERC-4337 wallets where execution is often bundled. While only authorized callers can invoke, attackers that obtain one signing capability (or a paymaster/EntryPoint pathway) could leverage this to degrade wallet availability or cause systematic failures in automated systems.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unbounded iteration over user-controlled array with external calls

preconditions: Caller is EntryPoint or an owner (onlyEntryPointOrOwner)
calls.length can be made very large within calldata size limits
Operational workflows depend on executeBatch succeeding atomically

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: gas exhaustion leading to persistent failure of intended batch operations
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no max batch size or gas-aware batching

analysis_questions: Is executeBatch used by protocol tooling expecting bounded size?
Can executeBatch be invoked by EntryPoint with attacker-controlled data via a userOp to grief wallet automation?
Are there any downstream integrations that assume executeBatch cannot be used for DoS?
validation_requirements: Fuzz calls.length to find practical failure thresholds
Measure gas per iteration and simulate bundler/EntryPoint constraints
Assess impact on typical wallet usage patterns

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker with signing capability submits a userOp calling executeBatch with thousands of calls. The operation consistently OOGs, causing repeated failures and preventing legitimate bundled actions if automation keeps retrying the same payload.
prerequisites: Some channel that repeatedly attempts attacker-controlled userOps (automation or compromised signer)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Indirect; can cause failed operations and wasted gas fees
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Wallet users relying on batching for critical workflows

threats.title: MagicSpend Paymaster Batch Balance Over-Commitment Leading to Solvency Violation
threats.hypothesis_description: In validatePaymasterUserOp, the balance check `address(this).balance < withdrawAmount` is performed independently per UserOperation. When a bundler includes multiple UserOps from the same or different senders using MagicSpend as paymaster, each validation independently passes the balance check against the same contract balance (which hasn't changed during validation). The cumulative _withdrawableETH can exceed the contract's actual ETH holdings. During postOp execution, the first postOp drains accumulated _withdrawableETH plus gas refund, leaving insufficient funds for subsequent postOps. The second postOp's forceSafeTransferETH may fail, triggering assert(mode != PostOpMode.postOpReverted) which consumes all remaining gas.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete solvency invariant violation. The balance check is per-operation, not cumulative. EntryPoint v0.6 runs all validatePaymasterUserOp calls sequentially before any execution/postOp. The contract's ETH balance doesn't change between validations since _withdrawableETH credits are purely state changes. This creates a TOCTOU gap between the balance check and actual fund transfers.

proof_of_signal.pattern: address(this).balance check is per-UserOp; _withdrawableETH[userOp.sender] += withdrawAmount - maxCost is additive across ops in same bundle

preconditions: MagicSpend contract holds ETH (e.g., 10 ETH)
Attacker has 2+ valid WithdrawRequests signed by MagicSpend owner (e.g., each for 7 ETH)
Bundler includes both UserOps in the same handleOps bundle

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Each `address(this).balance < withdrawAmount` check sees the SAME balance since no ETH is moved during validation
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Submit N UserOps where each individually passes the balance check but sum(withdrawAmount_i) > address(this).balance
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: First postOp receives all accumulated _withdrawableETH; later postOps fail, but attacker already extracted excess value. Total extracted can exceed contract balance.

economic_questions: Can a single sender have multiple valid WithdrawRequests in the same bundle?
How does EntryPoint v0.6 handle postOp failure when assert fires?
What is the exact sequence of postOp calls and which account gets the excess?
validation_requirements: Construct a test with MagicSpend holding N ETH and 2 UserOps each requesting > N/2 ETH
Verify that both pass validatePaymasterUserOp independently
Verify that the second postOp reverts due to insufficient balance

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas cost for bundling 2+ UserOps (~0.01 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to entire MagicSpend balance minus gas costs
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Up to 100% of over-committed amount (requires validation)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time MagicSpend has sufficient balance and attacker has multiple signed requests
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market impact - direct fund extraction from paymaster

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Users whose postOp fails lose their gas refund and withdrawal

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Contract balance, individual withdraw amounts, maxCost values
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace full lifecycle: validatePaymasterUserOp for all ops → execution → postOp for all ops

threats.title: Cross-Chain UUPS Upgrade Replay via executeWithoutChainIdValidation
threats.hypothesis_description: The canSkipChainIdValidation function returns true for UUPSUpgradeable.upgradeToAndCall.selector, allowing upgrade UserOperations to be replayed across all chains where the wallet exists at the same address. An attacker observes a legitimate upgrade on chain A (from on-chain data), then replays the identical UserOperation on chain B. If the implementation address contains different code on chain B (e.g., malicious contract deployed at same address via CREATE2, or no code at all), the wallet is compromised or bricked on chain B.
threats.investigation_rationale: The upgrade is whitelisted for chain-agnostic hash computation, meaning the signature is valid on any chain. The implementation address is deterministic but the CODE at that address can differ across chains. An attacker can deploy malicious code at the target implementation address on another chain using CREATE2 before the replay.

proof_of_signal.pattern: canSkipChainIdValidation returns true for UUPSUpgradeable.upgradeToAndCall.selector, enabling cross-chain replay of implementation upgrades

preconditions: Wallet deployed at same address on multiple chains
Owner executes a legitimate upgrade on chain A
Attacker can deploy different code at the target implementation address on chain B (via CREATE2 or EOA nonce manipulation)
Attacker can submit the replayed UserOp through a bundler on chain B

threats.title: Uninitialized Proxy Takeover via Empty Owners Array in initialize()
threats.hypothesis_description: CoinbaseSmartWallet.initialize() checks nextOwnerIndex() != 0 as its only re-initialization guard. If initialize is called with an empty owners array, _initializeOwners loops zero times, nextOwnerIndex remains 0, and the wallet remains re-initializable. Any subsequent caller can invoke initialize() with their own owner set. The factory enforces owners.length > 0, but the wallet's initialize() does not. Any proxy deployed outside the factory (manual ERC1967 proxy deployment) is vulnerable.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code has a clear gap: initialize() relies on nextOwnerIndex() != 0 as its initialization guard, but calling initialize([]) does not increment nextOwnerIndex. This is a code defect regardless of how the proxy is deployed, though exploitation requires non-factory deployment.
deployment_assumptions: Proxy deployed without using CoinbaseSmartWalletFactory (e.g., manual CREATE2 or third-party deployer)
threats.config_evidence: CoinbaseSmartWalletFactory.sol lines 44-45 enforce owners.length > 0 with OwnerRequired(), but CoinbaseSmartWallet.sol initialize() at lines 104-109 has no such check.

proof_of_signal.pattern: initialize() checks nextOwnerIndex() != 0 but calling with empty array leaves nextOwnerIndex at 0, allowing re-call

preconditions: Proxy deployed outside the factory (factory has owners.length > 0 guard)
initialize() called with empty array or not yet called
Attacker can submit a transaction to the proxy's initialize() before legitimate owner

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _initializeOwners(owners) loops `owners.length` times; with length 0, nextOwnerIndex is never incremented
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Call initialize([]) or find an uninitialized proxy, then call initialize([attacker_address]) to become sole owner
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Full wallet takeover — all current and future deposits are controlled by attacker

What is the total value in wallets that could be affected?

Call initialize([attacker]) on same proxy, confirm attacker becomes owner
Verify factory-deployed wallets are NOT affected

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Owner of the affected proxy wallet

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if any non-factory proxies exist on target chains
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm that initialize([]) leaves nextOwnerIndex == 0 via unit test

threats.title: MagicSpend postOp Assert Failure Creates Unrecoverable Fund Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: MagicSpend.postOp asserts `mode != PostOpMode.postOpReverted`. If the initial postOp call fails (e.g., forceSafeTransferETH fails due to insufficient contract balance from batch over-commitment in ECON-H001, or extreme gas conditions), the EntryPoint re-invokes postOp with PostOpMode.postOpReverted. The assert statement consumes all remaining gas, and the user's _withdrawableETH was already deleted in the first (reverted) postOp call, but the revert restored it. However, the UserOp's gas cost is still charged. The fundamental issue is that the assert creates an unrecoverable error mode that could lock user funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: The assert(mode != PostOpMode.postOpReverted) means that if the first postOp reverts for ANY reason, the second invocation with postOpReverted also fails. In EntryPoint v0.6, this could mean the user's withdrawal is lost because the EntryPoint charges gas but never successfully transfers the withdrawal.

proof_of_signal.pattern: assert(mode != PostOpMode.postOpReverted) creates hard failure; no graceful recovery path for failed ETH transfers

preconditions: The first postOp call must revert (insufficient balance from over-commitment, or extreme gas conditions)
EntryPoint calls postOp second time with postOpReverted mode

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: assert(mode != PostOpMode.postOpReverted) consumes ALL remaining gas with no recovery

threats.title: Cross-Chain Wallet Bricking via Upgrade Replay to Non-Existent Implementation
threats.hypothesis_description: The canSkipChainIdValidation function whitelists upgradeToAndCall.selector for cross-chain replay. A UserOp performing a UUPS upgrade via executeWithoutChainIdValidation is signed without chain ID binding. After an upgrade is executed on chain A, anyone can observe the on-chain transaction and replay the identical UserOp on chain B. If the new implementation address does not have deployed code on chain B (common when deployments are staggered across chains), the wallet proxy points to empty code, permanently bricking the wallet and locking all funds on chain B.
threats.investigation_rationale: The upgrade whitelist is an explicit design choice, but the asymmetric deployment risk across chains creates a window where legitimate upgrades become attack vectors. This is not hypothetical - cross-chain deployment ordering is a common operational reality.

proof_of_signal.pattern: canSkipChainIdValidation returns true for UUPSUpgradeable.upgradeToAndCall.selector, enabling cross-chain replay of implementation upgrades

preconditions: Wallet exists on multiple chains at the same address
Owner performs a UUPS upgrade via executeWithoutChainIdValidation on chain A
New implementation address has no code on chain B
Attacker observes the upgrade transaction on chain A (on-chain, not mempool)

initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires wallet owner to perform a legitimate upgrade on any chain
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Time between upgrade on chain A and implementation deployment on chain B

operational_questions: Is there a deployment procedure ensuring simultaneous implementation deployment across all target chains?
What happens to the proxy when implementation has no code - is it permanently bricked?
Can the upgrade be reversed on chain B if the implementation is later deployed?
validation_requirements: Test UUPS upgrade replay to an address with no code
Verify proxy behavior after upgrade to empty implementation
Confirm whether wallet can be recovered after bricking

threats.title: WebAuthn Signature Forgery via Spoofed RIP-7212 Precompile on Non-Supporting Chains
threats.hypothesis_description: WebAuthn.verify performs a staticcall to address 0x100 (the RIP-7212 precompile) before falling back to FCL. The check only verifies `ret.length > 0` and `abi.decode(ret, (uint256)) == 1`. On chains where a regular contract is deployed at address 0x100 (not the legitimate precompile), if that contract returns 32 bytes containing uint256(1) for arbitrary inputs, ALL passkey signature verifications would pass without actual cryptographic validation. This would allow any attacker to impersonate any passkey owner and drain all wallet funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: The wallet targets 8+ chains (Ethereum, Base, Optimism, Arbitrum, Polygon, BNB, Avalanche, Gnosis). Address 0x100 content varies across chains. If any current or future target chain has non-precompile code at this address, the entire passkey authentication is bypassed.
deployment_assumptions: Address 0x100 on all target chains is either empty or the legitimate RIP-7212 precompile
threats.config_evidence: WebAuthn.sol line 18: `address private constant VERIFIER = address(0x100)` - hardcoded without chain-specific validation

proof_of_signal.pattern: staticcall to VERIFIER (0x100) with only length check `ret.length > 0` and decode check `== 1`, no validation that response is from legitimate precompile

preconditions: Contract deployed on a chain where address 0x100 has non-precompile code
Code at 0x100 returns 32 bytes containing uint256(1) for staticcall inputs
Wallet has at least one passkey owner

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Precompile fallback logic at lines 145-153 trusts any contract at hardcoded address
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - purely dependent on chain environment
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent on any chain with spoofable 0x100 address

operational_questions: What code exists at address 0x100 on each of the 8 target chains?
Could future chain upgrades or forks deploy code at 0x100?
Does the staticcall result in valid-looking but incorrect verification on any chain?
validation_requirements: Audit address 0x100 on all target chains
Test WebAuthn.verify with arbitrary r,s values when 0x100 returns uint256(1)
Verify FCL fallback is never reached when 0x100 returns truthy data

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Verify that code at 0x100 returns 32 bytes with uint256(1) for arbitrary inputs
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Craft a WebAuthnAuth struct with arbitrary r,s values (no valid signature needed)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Submit UserOp or ERC-1271 signature impersonating any passkey owner to drain wallet

threats.title: Direct withdraw() permits arbitrary ERC20 withdrawals even though paymaster path enforces ETH-only (asset confusion drain)

If MagicSpend ever holds ERC20s (accidental transfers, sweeping, airdrops, or operational funding mistakes), an attacker with a valid owner-signed request (or any account receiving such a request) can withdraw those tokens. This breaks the operational assumption that MagicSpend only needs ETH liquidity and can create unexpected asset leakage paths.
threats.investigation_rationale: Realistic in production: contracts frequently receive stray ERC20s. The presence of a permissive direct withdraw path expands the asset-drain surface and complicates treasury controls and incident response.

threats.title: Unbounded owner index growth can create a gas-DoS vector for signature validation and owner management
threats.hypothesis_description: MultiOwnable uses a monotonically increasing nextOwnerIndex and never reuses removed indices. Over time, ownerAtIndex can become sparse with large indices. While signature validation uses a direct mapping lookup by index, operational tooling must track indices correctly; any attempt to 'scan' owners from 0..nextOwnerIndex for audits or to enforce policies becomes increasingly expensive. Additionally, large nextOwnerIndex values can increase calldata sizes and coordination complexity (e.g., if external systems store/communicate owner indices). This can be weaponized by an owner to grief operational processes by repeatedly adding/removing owners to inflate nextOwnerIndex.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an operational DoS risk that a real adversary with minimal privileges (any owner) can execute without needing MEV. It can break monitoring, compliance, or recovery procedures that depend on enumerating owner indices.

proof_of_signal.pattern: nextOwnerIndex++ on every add; removeOwnerAtIndex deletes slot without reuse; no compaction

preconditions: Attacker controls at least one owner key (or gains it temporarily)
Operational tooling depends on enumerating owners by scanning indices or assumes small ownerIndex values
Wallet expected to remain functional over long lifecycle (many owner changes)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Index is the primary identifier in SignatureWrapper; sparse mapping and ever-growing indices complicate coordination and monitoring.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Any single owner can perform the grief sequence.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Over multiple transactions; persistent once index is inflated.

threats.title: ERC1271 domain separator recomputation is not cached and may be used as a gas-grief vector in high-frequency signature checks
threats.hypothesis_description: ERC1271.domainSeparator() recomputes keccak256(name), keccak256(version), and the domain separator on every call, and is indirectly used by replaySafeHash() and isValidSignature(). If third-party protocols call isValidSignature repeatedly (e.g., on-chain orderbooks, permit-like flows, batched validations), an attacker can force additional gas consumption by triggering repeated validations (e.g., by submitting many small interactions that cause signature checks). This increases costs and can make integrations brittle.
threats.investigation_rationale: Wallets are often used as signers in other on-chain systems. Gas-griefing via repeated ERC1271 checks is practical and can be exploited without MEV, especially in systems where anyone can trigger signature verification (e.g., verifying off-chain quotes).

proof_of_signal.pattern: domainSeparator() recomputes keccak256 over name/version each call; no caching by chainid

preconditions: A third-party contract frequently calls CoinbaseSmartWallet.isValidSignature() as part of a public state-changing path (not just view calls)
Attacker can trigger that path repeatedly at low cost (e.g., spam small orders/requests)
Gas costs are a meaningful DoS vector for the integration

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No caching pattern (common in EIP-712) despite immutable name/version.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None; depends on external integration behavior.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever integrations expose public entry points that validate signatures.

operational_questions: Which integrated protocols call isValidSignature in non-view, user-triggerable paths?

Is there a recommended off-chain validation path to avoid on-chain checks?

threats.title: MagicSpend Paymaster Violates ERC-7562 Storage Access Rules, Causing Bundler Rejection
threats.hypothesis_description: The `validatePaymasterUserOp` function checks `address(this).balance` to ensure sufficient funds. However, ERC-7562 strictly prohibits accessing the paymaster's own balance during the validation phase to prevent unpredictable simulation results. Standard ERC-4337 bundlers will automatically reject any UserOp utilizing this paymaster, causing a complete denial of service on public mempools.
threats.investigation_rationale: ERC-4337 bundlers enforce strict storage access rules during the validation phase to prevent DoS attacks against the mempool. Accessing `address(this).balance` is explicitly banned because the balance can change outside of the UserOp's control (e.g., via direct ETH transfers), invalidating the simulation.

threats.title: MagicSpend cumulative solvency break: multiple validations can over-credit _withdrawableETH beyond available ETH, enabling later drains of future deposits

The code performs no global or cumulative accounting reservation, so it is plausible that multiple validations can create a temporary or persistent solvency hole that can be monetized.

threats.title: FCL.ecAff_isOnCurve Accepts Non-Canonical P-256 Points Enabling Persistent Owner Backdoor
threats.hypothesis_description: FCL.ecAff_isOnCurve does not reject coordinates x > p or y > p (except exact equality x == p or y == p). Since all arithmetic uses mulmod with p, a point (x+p, y) is mathematically identical to (x, y) but encodes to different bytes via abi.encode. MultiOwnable.addOwnerPublicKey stores the raw bytes without canonicalization, and _addOwnerAtIndex only checks exact byte equality for deduplication (AlreadyOwner). An existing owner can register equivalent non-canonical public keys as separate owners. When one is removed via removeOwnerAtIndex, the other persists, allowing the removed owner to retain full wallet control.
threats.investigation_rationale: This combines a missing input validation in the cryptographic library with an encoding-level deduplication gap in the owner registry to create a persistent backdoor that survives explicit owner removal, potentially draining all wallet funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: ecAff_isOnCurve rejects x==p and y==p but NOT x>p or y>p; mulmod(x,x,p) reduces x mod p, so non-canonical coordinates pass the curve check

preconditions: Attacker is an existing wallet owner (EOA or passkey)
Attacker generates a P-256 keypair where x < 2^256 - p (approximately x < 2^224, achievable with ~2^32 key generation attempts)
No external monitoring system checks for modular equivalence of newly added public keys

threats.title: CoinbaseSmartWalletFactory.createAccount Permanently Locks ETH for Already-Deployed Accounts
threats.hypothesis_description: When createAccount is called with msg.value > 0 for an already-deployed wallet (alreadyDeployed == true from LibClone.createDeterministicERC1967), the CREATE2 instruction fails silently because the address is occupied. The msg.value ETH is NOT sent to the existing wallet and instead remains in the factory contract, which has no withdrawal function. The ETH is permanently locked.
threats.investigation_rationale: This represents a direct, permanent loss of user funds through a normal-looking factory interaction. Users or front-ends that attempt to fund a wallet during creation when the wallet already exists will lose their ETH irrecoverably.

proof_of_signal.pattern: LibClone.createDeterministicERC1967(msg.value, implementation, salt) passes msg.value to CREATE2; if already deployed, CREATE2 fails and value stays in factory; factory has no withdraw function

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: ETH is permanently locked, not stolen (griefing attack). On no-mempool chains, attacker would need to predict owner/nonce combinations.

threats.title: MultiOwnable.addOwnerPublicKey Lacks P-256 Curve Point Validation Enabling Invalid Owner Slot Consumption
threats.hypothesis_description: addOwnerPublicKey accepts arbitrary bytes32 x and y values without validating that (x, y) is a valid point on the P-256 curve. Invalid public keys are stored as 64-byte owners and consume owner index slots. Signatures against these invalid keys always fail (FCL.ecdsa_verify returns false because ecAff_isOnCurve fails), making the owner slot permanently non-functional. A malicious owner can pollute the owner registry with invalid keys, degrading wallet management and potentially confusing off-chain systems.
threats.investigation_rationale: Missing input validation at the registration boundary creates dead owner slots. While not directly exploitable for fund theft, it degrades the security model and could be used for griefing in multi-owner scenarios.

preconditions: Attacker is an existing wallet owner
No off-chain validation of public key validity before addOwnerPublicKey

threats.title: MagicSpend._withdraw ERC20 Fee-on-Transfer Token Accounting Discrepancy
threats.hypothesis_description: MagicSpend._withdraw uses SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer for ERC20 tokens, transferring the full `amount` specified in the WithdrawRequest. If the configured asset is a fee-on-transfer token, the recipient receives less than `amount` while the signed WithdrawRequest authorizes `amount`. The MagicSpend owner may sign multiple WithdrawRequests totaling more than the actual token balance because they assume each withdrawal transfers the full amount, but fees erode the real balance faster than expected.
threats.investigation_rationale: The withdraw function and _withdraw internal function handle arbitrary ERC20 tokens but perform no accounting for transfer fees, creating a discrepancy between authorized amounts and actual amounts received/deducted.

threats.title: Selective Cross-Chain Replay of Owner Management Enables Permanent Unauthorized Access
threats.hypothesis_description: The `executeWithoutChainIdValidation` function allows replaying owner additions and removals across chains. threats.title: MagicSpend paymaster nonce-burning DoS: any EntryPoint caller can permanently invalidate signed withdraw requests by submitting failing UserOps
threats.hypothesis_description: MagicSpend consumes (marks used) the withdrawRequest nonce inside validatePaymasterUserOp before signature validity/expiry is enforced by EntryPoint acceptance. An attacker can submit a UserOperation that references a victim's intended WithdrawRequest (same account+nonce) but with an invalid signature (or expired request). Even if EntryPoint rejects the operation (validationData indicates failure/expiry), the nonce remains burned on-chain, forcing the victim to obtain a newly signed request.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an on-chain griefing vector against a production paymaster that can be executed repeatedly and cheaply if withdraw requests are observable (e.g., via an API or logs). It directly degrades availability and user experience and can increase operational load (reissuing signatures).

threats.title: CoinbaseSmartWallet replay-safe ERC1271 hashing may break dApp expectations leading to unsafe fallback signing patterns
threats.hypothesis_description: ERC1271.isValidSignature validates signatures over replaySafeHash(hash) (EIP-712 wrapping with this wallet's domain). DApps that assume the wallet validates the raw hash may experience signature failures and implement unsafe fallbacks (e.g., accepting an EOA signature from one of the owners instead of requiring wallet validation). This can lead to authorization bypass in integrated systems if they accidentally accept signatures not bound to the wallet address.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is an integration-driven operational threat: the code is correct for replay protection, but the deviation from many wallets' ERC-1271 behavior can induce downstream security failures. Real attackers exploit integration mismatches frequently.
deployment_assumptions: A downstream dApp verifies permissions via ERC-1271 but incorrectly handles failures by falling back to weaker verification
The dApp does not compute replaySafeHash when requesting signatures from users
threats.config_evidence: No integration inventory provided; ERC1271 code shows replaySafeHash wrapper applied unconditionally.

proof_of_signal.pattern: isValidSignature(): _validateSignature({message: replaySafeHash(hash), ...})

threats.hypothesis_description: Anyone can deploy a wallet for a given (owners, nonce) set via createAccount and thus materialize the deterministic address on-chain before the intended user does. While ownership remains correct (initialized with provided owners), this can be used operationally to misdirect deposits: third parties may send funds to a predicted address assuming the user controls deployment timing or has verified deployment status. Attackers can deploy 'for' users en masse, making it harder to distinguish legitimate deployments and potentially enabling phishing-like onchain confusions without mempool reliance.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a realistic operational issue in deterministic-account ecosystems: address existence can be forced by third parties. It can break workflows that assume 'account not deployed yet' or rely on deployment as an explicit user action.

Offchain systems treat contract creation as a trust signal (e.g., only accept deposits after self-deploy)

threats.title: FCL ecZZ_mulmuladd_S_asm Incorrect Point-at-Infinity Handling During Scalar Multiplication
threats.hypothesis_description: In the main loop of ecZZ_mulmuladd_S_asm, when the accumulated point and the point to be added are inverses of each other (same x, negated y), the condition y2==0 && T2==0 (the doubling special case) is NOT triggered. Instead, the code proceeds with normal point addition using T2=0 (the 'P' value), leading to division by zero in projective coordinates (mulmod by 0). This produces mathematically incorrect intermediate results that could potentially lead to a false positive in ECDSA verification.
threats.investigation_rationale: The assembly code has a comment '//special extremely rare case accumulator where EcAdd is replaced by EcDbl' and '//todo : construct edge vector case', indicating the authors are aware of the edge case but have not fully handled the inverse-point scenario. When T2=0 but y2!=0, the points are additive inverses, and the result should be the point at infinity. Instead, the calculation produces garbage values. For P-256 with prime order, this case is astronomically rare for random inputs but could potentially be targeted by crafting specific (r,s) signature values.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if iszero(y2) { if iszero(T2) { /* doubling */ continue } } — only handles y2==0 && T2==0 (point doubling); T2==0 && y2!=0 (point inverse) falls through to normal addition with T2=0, causing incorrect computation

preconditions: During Strauss-Shamir double-and-add, the accumulated point coincides with the negative of {G, Q, or G+Q}
Attacker crafts (r, s) values that trigger this specific intermediate state
The incorrect computation produces a final x-coordinate that matches r

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing point-at-infinity case in projective addition; TODO comment acknowledges incomplete handling
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: When accumulated point = -(point to add), T2=0 causes mulmod(T2,T2,p)=0, leading to zero PPP, zero ZZ update, garbage X and Y values
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No explicit check for T2==0 && y2!=0 (inverse point / point at infinity result)

analysis_questions: Can specific (r, s, Qx, Qy) values be crafted to trigger the inverse-point case during the Strauss-Shamir loop?
When triggered, what does the incorrect computation produce?
Can the final x-coordinate of the garbage computation accidentally equal r, creating a false positive?
validation_requirements: Construct test vectors where intermediate point equals negative of {G, Q, G+Q}

Attempt to find (r, s) values that produce false positive through this path

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker reverse-engineers (r, s) values for a given public key Q such that during iteration i of the Strauss-Shamir loop, the accumulated point equals -G (or -Q or -(G+Q)). The incorrect addition produces garbage projective coordinates. After the remaining iterations and final affine conversion, if the x-coordinate happens to equal r, the invalid signature is accepted, granting the attacker wallet control.
prerequisites: Ability to find specific (r, s) triggering the edge case

threats.title: WebAuthn requireUV Hardcoded to False Reduces Passkey Security to User Presence Only
threats.hypothesis_description: CoinbaseSmartWallet._validateSignature hardcodes requireUV: false when calling WebAuthn.verify. This means the User Verified flag in authenticatorData is never checked, allowing signatures from authenticators that performed only user presence verification (physical tap) without user verification (biometric/PIN). This reduces the security of passkey-owned wallets from two-factor to single-factor for physical access scenarios.
threats.investigation_rationale: With requireUV=false, an attacker with physical access to an unlocked device containing the passkey can authorize any wallet operation with just a tap, without biometric or PIN verification. For wallets controlling significant funds, this is a meaningful reduction in security guarantees.

threats.title: ERC1271 replaySafeHash domainSeparator recomputation per call may enable griefing via expensive signature checks (gas DoS) in external integrations
threats.hypothesis_description: ERC1271.domainSeparator recomputes keccak hashes of name/version strings each call, and isValidSignature performs replaySafeHash then calls wallet’s signature verification, which for WebAuthn can be very expensive (sha256 + possible FCL modexp). Third-party protocols that call isValidSignature in state-changing flows (e.g., order fills, permission checks) could be griefed by causing repeated expensive validations, potentially pushing transactions over gas limits or making some integrations impractical.
threats.investigation_rationale: Not a direct fund loss bug, but a practical integration risk: ERC-1271 is often invoked by other contracts during critical operations. If verification is consistently expensive, an attacker may cause DoS of those operations by forcing signature paths to WebAuthn/FCL frequently.
deployment_assumptions: A downstream protocol calls wallet.isValidSignature() inside state-changing paths and does not cap gas tightly

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: On a chain where 0x100 is an always-true verifier contract, attacker submits a UserOp signed with arbitrary WebAuthnAuth. validateUserOp accepts it as valid, allowing attacker to execute transfers or add themselves as owner.

Wallet uses WebAuthn owners

threats.title: CoinbaseSmartWalletFactory createAccount salt depends on raw owners bytes ordering; duplicate semantic owner sets can create multiple addresses, breaking user expectations and recovery tooling
threats.hypothesis_description: Factory salt is keccak256(abi.encode(owners, nonce)) where owners is a bytes[] array. The same set of owners in different order yields different wallet addresses; additionally, different byte encodings that are semantically equivalent (e.g., address(0) vs malformed 32 bytes within uint160 range) may produce different addresses. This can be exploited to confuse users or integrators into deploying 'lookalike' wallets and splitting funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: Not a direct on-chain theft, but a practical operational attack surface: if off-chain systems assume owner set is order-independent, an attacker can induce users to fund an unintended address with same owners but different ordering/encoding. Given the constraints, this is more of a correctness/UX risk but can lead to loss of funds if users deposit to the wrong address.
deployment_assumptions: Integrations compute or display predicted addresses from owner sets without canonical sorting/normalization
Users or apps might fund the predicted address assuming set semantics rather than ordered list semantics
threats.config_evidence: No documentation states owners are sorted/canonicalized; factory uses raw abi.encode of owners array.

proof_of_signal.pattern: salt derived from ordered, non-canonical bytes[] input

preconditions: Victim/integration relies on predicted address from owners set
Attacker can influence ordering/encoding presented to victim (e.g., via API suggestions, client-side bugs, or multi-platform mismatch)
Victim funds the wrong derived address

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: no canonicalization of owner list before address derivation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: address derivation ambiguity; potential fund loss via confusion
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no sorting, no normalization, no unique set enforcement at factory level

analysis_questions: Do official clients sort/normalize owners before calling getAddress/createAccount?
Are there multiple encodings accepted by initialize/_initializeOwners that represent the same owner?
Is there any UI/API that claims address depends only on set, not order?
validation_requirements: Demonstrate two different owner orderings produce two different addresses
Check whether MultiOwnable accepts multiple encodings for same address owner (should not, but verify encoding constraints end-to-end)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker convinces a user/integration to compute a wallet address using owners in a different order than the one used when deploying (or uses a different client that orders differently). User sends funds to the mismatched predicted address; funds are controlled by same owners but may not be monitored/recovered by tooling expecting the canonical address.
prerequisites: Cross-client inconsistency or integration bug
User funds the computed address

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: User funds misdirected to an unintended account address; recovery depends on owners noticing and accessing it
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users using multiple platforms/integrations to derive wallet addresses
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Possible if owners control both accounts, but operationally error-prone

threats.test_coverage_gap: No tests asserting canonical owner ordering requirements or documenting them

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: identify multiple deployed wallets sharing same owner set in different orders

threats.title: Bundler Griefing via Cumulative Balance Check Bypass in Paymaster
threats.hypothesis_description: The `validatePaymasterUserOp` function checks if the paymaster's balance covers the `withdrawAmount` for each UserOp independently. An attacker can submit a bundle of UserOps that individually pass this check but collectively exceed the paymaster's balance. During execution, the `postOp` transfers will fail, causing the EntryPoint to revert the entire bundle and slash the bundler's gas.
threats.investigation_rationale: This violates the economic conservation invariant of the paymaster. By bypassing the cumulative balance check during validation, the attacker forces the failure into the execution phase (`postOp`). In ERC-4337, execution phase failures caused by the paymaster result in the bundler paying the gas without compensation, creating a severe griefing vector against the bundler network.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (address(this).balance < withdrawAmount) revert InsufficientBalance(...)

preconditions: Attacker possesses multiple valid `WithdrawRequest` signatures from the MagicSpend owner
The sum of the `withdrawAmount`s exceeds the MagicSpend contract's ETH balance
The attacker submits these UserOps in a single bundle to a bundler

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Independent balance check instead of cumulative tracking during validation
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker constructs a bundle where `sum(withdrawAmount) > balance`. Validation passes. `postOp` fails on the Nth UserOp due to insufficient balance, triggering the `assert` in `postOpReverted` mode.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker inflicts financial damage on a competing bundler (gas costs) at zero cost to themselves

economic_questions: Do bundlers simulate the entire bundle cumulatively, which would catch this before submission?
Can the attacker front-run the bundle with a direct `withdraw` to artificially lower the balance after simulation?
validation_requirements: Construct a bundle with two UserOps that exceed the balance
Observe the EntryPoint's behavior when the second `postOp` reverts

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Cost inflicted on victim bundler (~0.05 ETH per bundle)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When the MagicSpend balance is low but non-zero

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of gas fees for the reverted bundle
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Ongoing gas costs until the attacker exhausts their valid signatures

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm that `forceSafeTransferETH` reverts when balance is insufficient

threats.title: MagicSpend Admin Functions Can Drain ETH Committed to User _withdrawableETH Balances
threats.hypothesis_description: MagicSpend.entryPointDeposit, entryPointAddStake, and ownerWithdraw all transfer ETH from the contract balance without checking against the sum of _withdrawableETH commitments. If _withdrawableETH credits exist (between validatePaymasterUserOp and postOp), these admin functions can drain the backing ETH, causing postOp's forceSafeTransferETH to fail. The postOp failure triggers assert(mode != PostOpMode.postOpReverted), consuming all remaining gas and permanently losing the user's credited funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: The admin functions and the paymaster flow share the same ETH balance but have no mutual accounting. This creates a race between admin operations and in-flight UserOp lifecycle, potentially violating the solvency invariant.

proof_of_signal.pattern: entryPointDeposit uses SafeTransferLib.safeTransferETH(entryPoint(), amount) without checking amount <= address(this).balance - sum(_withdrawableETH)

preconditions: MagicSpend owner calls entryPointDeposit/entryPointAddStake/ownerWithdraw
There are in-flight UserOps with credited _withdrawableETH balances
Admin withdrawal amount exceeds balance minus committed _withdrawableETH

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Contract balance = 10 ETH; _withdrawableETH[user] = 8 ETH; entryPointDeposit(5 ETH) → balance = 5 ETH < 8 ETH commitment
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin drains balance below _withdrawableETH commitments; postOp fails; user loses credited funds
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Not direct profit for admin, but creates unbacked _withdrawableETH → user fund loss

economic_questions: What is the time window between validatePaymasterUserOp and postOp during which _withdrawableETH is vulnerable?
Can a malicious admin time their withdrawal to coincide with in-flight UserOps?
Does the EntryPoint's simulation prevent this during bundle validation?
validation_requirements: Simulate: validate UserOp (credits _withdrawableETH), admin calls entryPointDeposit draining balance, then postOp attempts transfer
Verify assert failure behavior in postOp when forceSafeTransferETH fails
Check if EntryPoint's simulation catches this race condition

preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Must coincide with in-flight UserOp lifecycle

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During high-volume paymaster usage with large _withdrawableETH balances
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could cascade to bundler reputation loss for MagicSpend

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Map the lifecycle timing between validatePaymasterUserOp credits and postOp debits
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Determine if EntryPoint v0.6 prevents concurrent admin operations during UserOp processing

threats.title: Single-Factor Authentication for Passkeys via Disabled User Verification
threats.hypothesis_description: `CoinbaseSmartWallet` hardcodes `requireUV: false` when calling `WebAuthn.verify`, disabling biometric/PIN checks. An attacker with physical access to an unlocked device can authorize arbitrary transactions by simply tapping the screen, reducing security to single-factor.
threats.investigation_rationale: Disabling user verification significantly weakens the security guarantees of passkeys, making physical device compromise a critical threat.

proof_of_signal.pattern: `WebAuthn.verify({..., requireUV: false, ...})`

preconditions: User has a passkey owner registered
Attacker gains physical access to the user's unlocked device

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Biometric/PIN verification is explicitly bypassed in the signature validation.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: While device is unlocked and unattended.

operational_questions: Is `requireUV: false` a strict requirement for intended UX?
Can users opt-in to require User Verification?
validation_requirements: Generate a WebAuthn signature without biometric verification and verify it passes on-chain.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Gain physical access to victim's unlocked phone/computer.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Initiate a transaction to transfer funds or add an attacker-controlled owner.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Tap the authenticator prompt (no biometrics required).

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High, attacker can remove legitimate owners.

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected large transfers or owner changes.

threats.title: MagicSpend Admin Can Drain Funds Backing Valid User Withdrawals
threats.hypothesis_description: The `ownerWithdraw` function allows the admin to withdraw any amount of ETH, including the funds that are currently backing users' valid `WithdrawRequest` signatures. This can render valid user signatures useless and trap user funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: The lack of accounting for outstanding withdrawal obligations allows the admin to rug-pull users or accidentally cause insolvency.

threats.title: FCL modular inversion via ModExp precompile: chain-specific precompile behavior or gas limits can cause signature validation outages
threats.hypothesis_description: FCL uses the ModExp precompile (0x05) for modular inversion in both FCL_nModInv and FCL_pModInv. If a target chain has non-standard ModExp gas schedule, implementation quirks, or limits that make these calls fail for certain inputs, WebAuthn verification fallback (and any use of FCL.ecdsa_verify) may revert, causing wallet signature validation failures and potentially bricking passkey-based accounts operationally.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a production availability risk across heterogeneous chains listed as targets. Attackers may not control precompile behavior, but outages can be triggered by chain upgrades or misconfiguration and manifest as widespread auth failures.
deployment_assumptions: Wallets rely on WebAuthn->FCL fallback path on chains without RIP-7212 verifier
Target chain's ModExp precompile is present but behaves unexpectedly or has restrictive gas/size constraints
threats.config_evidence: No chain-specific deployment evidence provided; dependency is hardcoded to precompile 0x05 in FCL.

proof_of_signal.pattern: staticcall(not(0), 0x05, pointer, 0xc0, pointer, 0x20) { revert(0,0) }

preconditions: Chain does not support RIP-7212 (0x100) or precompile returns false, forcing FCL fallback
ModExp precompile is unreliable or can be made to fail due to gas constraints in the calling context
Wallet operations depend on passkey validation

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Hard dependency on precompile 0x05; failures revert rather than returning false
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During chain upgrades/outages or under extreme gas constraints

operational_questions: On each target chain, what is the ModExp gas schedule and are there known divergences?
Are there upper bounds on gas available during validateUserOp that could make ModExp fail?
Is there monitoring for increased revert rates in validateUserOp attributable to ModExp calls?
validation_requirements: Cross-chain test suite running WebAuthn verification fallback on each target chain/fork
Measure gas usage and failure modes of ModExp staticcalls under realistic validation gas limits

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify chain where ModExp behaves unexpectedly or becomes expensive
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Trigger wallet operations requiring FCL inversion (passkey validations) under tight gas constraints
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Observe systematic validation failures / inability to use passkey owners

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Medium: passkey accounts unable to transact; auth outage
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High: may require chain upgrade, alternate verification path, or account owner migration

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: During chain instability or validation gas limit constraints

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Spike in reverts from FCL_nModInv/FCL_pModInv callsites
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Increased validateUserOp failures specifically for passkey owners

threats.title: CoinbaseSmartWallet EOA-owner signature verification relies on SignatureCheckerLib: contract-based owners could introduce unexpected reverts during validateUserOp
threats.hypothesis_description: When an owner is an Ethereum address (32-byte ownerBytes), _validateSignature uses SignatureCheckerLib.isValidSignatureNow(owner, message, signatureData). If the owner address is a smart contract that reverts or behaves unexpectedly for ERC-1271 checks, validateUserOp can revert instead of returning validationData=1, potentially causing wallet operations to fail in a way that bundlers treat as hard failure. This becomes operationally relevant if owners are set to contract accounts (e.g., guardians, session keys) that can change behavior over time.
threats.investigation_rationale: Wallet availability depends on external contract owners behaving correctly. This is a realistic operational hazard when users add contract owners (multisigs, modules).
deployment_assumptions: Users add contract addresses as owners (supported by addOwnerAddress)
Those contracts implement ERC-1271 in a way that can revert or become non-functional
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence; behavior depends on what owners are added.

proof_of_signal.pattern: SignatureCheckerLib.isValidSignatureNow(owner, message, sigWrapper.signatureData)

preconditions: At least one registered owner is a contract address
That contract reverts or consumes excessive gas on signature checks
Wallet relies on that ownerIndex being used for signing or attackers can force it via malformed SignatureWrapper (leading to revert)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: External contract call during validation can revert; wallet does not catch and convert to soft-fail
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires owner management to remove/replace problematic owner
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Whenever contract-owner signature path is used

operational_questions: Is it intended/supported to add contract addresses as owners?
Does SignatureCheckerLib bubble up reverts or handle them gracefully in this version?
Do bundlers treat reverts during validateUserOp as a different class of failure vs validationData=1?
validation_requirements: Add a reverting ERC-1271 contract as owner and attempt validateUserOp to observe whether it reverts
Confirm whether failure results in permanent account usability issues or only affects that ownerIndex

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Induce/identify a contract-owner that reverts on signature checks (environmental/owner choice)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Cause wallet operations to rely on that ownerIndex, triggering repeated validation reverts and DoS
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Low for attacker if user already configured such an owner; otherwise requires user action

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Medium: UserOps fail hard; wallet may become difficult to use until owner set updated
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium: requires using another ownerIndex to remove the bad owner
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: ERC-4337 flows and any ERC-1271 based validations

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time after contract-owner is added

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: validateUserOp revert traces involving calls to owner contract
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Wallet suddenly unable to validate signatures after an owner contract upgrade
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Failures tied to specific ownerIndex selections
